content.problemStatement:
You are in the year 2050 and many things have changed. Of course, there are flying cars and vacations on Mars are common, but it's time to focus on the most important thing of all: IOI selections.

IOI selections now only allow each country to send one student to IOI. Now, since you're no longer a student, you've decided to organise the selection rounds to choose the student who will go to IOI from Siruseri.

You organise two contests. Each contest is out of PPP points, and a participant can therefore score any integer score from 111 to PPP, both inclusive. There are NNN participants, numbered from 111 to NNN, and each participant takes part in both of these contests.

Now, you have the scores from every participant. There is just one problem though: you are not sure how you will combine the scores from the two contests, in order to select the best participants!

Therefore, you're now required to choose any real number rrr, such that 0≤r≤1000 \leq r \leq 1000≤r≤100. Then, if some participant has a score of AAA on the first contest, and a score of BBB on the second contest, their composite score will become Ar+B(100−r)100\frac{Ar + B(100-r)}{100}100Ar+B(100−r)​. Finally, you will choose the student with the largest composite score to be Siruseri's representative at IOI. If there is more than one student with the same largest composite score, you are allowed choose any one of these students as the representative.

Now, you want to find out the answer to one simple question: which of the students can become the representatives of Siruseri, assuming that you can choose any real value of rrr between 000 and 100100100, both inclusive? Again, if there are ties for the maximum composite score, you are also allowed to pick which of the tied students is the representative.


Input Format
The first line of input contains two space-separated integers, NNN and PPP. NNN is the number of students taking part, and PPP is the maximum possible score on the test.


The following NNN lines of input each contain two space-separated integers, representing the scores of the students. The ithi^{th}ith line contains AiA_iAi​ and BiB_iBi​, where AiA_iAi​ is the score of the ithi^{th}ith participant on the first contest, and BiB_iBi​ is the score of the ithi^{th}ith participant on the second contest. It is guaranteed that 1≤Ai,Bi≤P1 \leq A_i, B_i \leq P1≤Ai​,Bi​≤P for all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N.



Output Format
On the first line of output, print a single integer KKK: the number of possible representatives of Siruseri.


On the second line of output, print KKK space-separated integers, the list of possible representatives of Siruseri. This list should be printed in ascending order, and should have no duplicates.
constraints: For all subtasks, 1≤N≤2000001 \leq N \leq 2000001≤N≤200000 and 1≤P≤1071 \leq P \leq 10^71≤P≤107. Additionally, 1≤Ai,Bi≤P1 \leq A_i, B_i \leq P1≤Ai​,Bi​≤P for all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N.
Subtask 3 [8 points]: For all students, either Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1 or Bi=1B_i = 1Bi​=1 (or possibly both).

Subtask 4 [18 points]: It is sufficient to consider only integer values of rrr. Formally, if a student can be selected as the representative using some value of rrr, there exists an integer value of rrr such that they can be selected as the representative.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first sample, choosing r=0r = 0r=0 will make the cumulative scores:

Person 1: 8
Person 2: 8
Person 3: 5
Person 4: 4
Person 5: 5
Person 6: 6

Both the 1st person and the 2nd person are potential first placers, if r=0r = 0r=0 is chosen.
Meanwhile, choosing r=100r = 100r=100 will make the cumulative scores:

Person 1: 1
Person 2: 5
Person 3: 2
Person 4: 5
Person 5: 2
Person 6: 5

The 2nd person, 4th person and 6th person are potential first placers, if r=100r = 100r=100 is chosen.
It can be shown that regardless of the value of rrr you choose, the 3rd and 5th people can never be first place. So there are four people who can be first, so we output 4. On the next line, we print the list of four people who can be first: 1, 2, 4 and 6.

Explanation:
For the second sample, choosing r=7.28r = 7.28r=7.28 will allow the 3rd and 5th people to be first. Alternatively, choosing r=88.89r = 88.89r=88.89 will allow the 2nd person to be first. Therefore, the 2nd, 3rd and 5th people are the list of people who can be first.

Explanation:
For the third sample, regardless of what weightage you choose, the third person is the only one who can come first. So the answer is just the person 3.

Explanation:
For the fourth sample, if you choose r=2r = 2r=2, person 1 can be first. If you choose r=99r = 99r=99,  person 2 can be first. If you choose r=43r = 43r=43, person 3 can be first. So everyone can be first, so we output all three people.
